coredata.dc:title: Spectrofluorometric genotyping of single nucleotide polymorphisms using carbon dots as fluorophores
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Motaghi, Hasan
Mehrgardi, Masoud Ayatollahi
coredata.dc:description: In the present manuscript, a new spectrofluorometric method for the genotyping of various single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) using carbon dots (CDs) is investigated. For the construction of the assay, thiolated probe DNA is self-assembled on a gold surface via sulfur‑gold chemistry and afterward, the probe is partially hybridized with a longer target DNA strand. Subsequently, the unhybridized section of the target DNA is hybridized with a capture DNA to form the DNA double-helix self-assembled monolayer on the gold surface. Finally, CDs surface amine groups are covalently attached to the 5′ phosphate groups of various monobases (MB-CDs) using phosphoramidite chemistry. In this method, genotyping of SNPs is based on following the changes in fluorescence intensity of the MB-CDs suspensions before and after incubation with DNA modified gold surface. The assay is straightforward with no need for target labeling and is sensitive and low cost enough to genotype various SNPs independent of their position in a DNA double helix with an acceptable limit of detections in picomolar ranges.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single nucleotide polymorphism
Genotyping
Carbon dots
Spectrofluorometry

Preparation of Monobase Functionalized Carbon Dots (MB-CDs)
Hybridization Between Mismatched Sites of Duplex DNA and MB-CDs
Designing and Fabrication of the Assay
Preparation of Monobase Functionalized Carbon Dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this study by the Research Council of the University of Isfahan .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EIS spectra (0.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 −, 10 kHz to 0.1 Hz) solution for each step of gold surface modification.
FTIR spectra of CD, T-CD, G-CD, C-CD and A-CD.
The changes in the fluorescence intensities versus incubation times of MB-CD with the double helix on the gold surface.
The changes in the fluorescence intensities ΔF of the MB-CDs suspensions incubated with the modified gold surface, using H1, H2, and complementary strands.
Fluorescence spectra and ΔF versus H1(A) and H2(B) concentrations (calibration curves).
Schematic illustration of Spectrofluorometric Genotyping of various SNPs.
Linear ranges and detection limits for different concentrations of A-C (H1) and G-T (H2) mismatch targets.

head.ce:title: Spectrofluorometric genotyping of single nucleotide polymorphisms using carbon dots as fluorophores

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Isfahan, Isfahan 81746-73441, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present manuscript, a new spectrofluorometric method for the genotyping of various single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) using carbon dots (CDs) is investigated. For the construction of the assay, thiolated probe DNA is self-assembled on a gold surface via sulfur‑gold chemistry and afterward, the probe is partially hybridized with a longer target DNA strand. Subsequently, the unhybridized section of the target DNA is hybridized with a capture DNA to form the DNA double-helix self-assembled monolayer on the gold surface. Finally, CDs surface amine groups are covalently attached to the 5′ phosphate groups of various monobases (MB-CDs) using phosphoramidite chemistry. In this method, genotyping of SNPs is based on following the changes in fluorescence intensity of the MB-CDs suspensions before and after incubation with DNA modified gold surface. The assay is straightforward with no need for target labeling and is sensitive and low cost enough to genotype various SNPs independent of their position in a DNA double helix with an acceptable limit of detections in picomolar ranges.

row.entry: A new spectrofluorometric method for the genotyping of various single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) using carbon dots (CDs)
Name and reference of original method

ce:section.ce:para: Variations of a single DNA base, occurring at approximately one per 500 to 1000 bp in the human genome, are called single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP). This change in the somatic cells may lead to an inherited or a non-inherited genetic disease [1]. The transmission of the genetic code entirely depends on the specific pairings of adenine (A) with thymine (T), and cytosine (C) with guanine (G), as described in the early 1950s by Watson and Crick [2]. The pairing of purine with the wrong pyrimidine can lead to different base mismatch types. The important difficulty of SNP genotyping protocols is that many combinations can be formed even with the alteration of a single base. In fact, eight possible SNPs including, A-C, A-A, A-G, C-C, C-T, T-T, T-G, and G-G can be formed [3]. Many pathogenic and genetic diseases such as cystic fibrosis, Alzheimer's, sickle cell anemia and certain cancers caused by these point mutations. The development of a reliable and simple method for the identification of single nucleotide polymorphisms is an important research area for diagnosis of disease-causing genes and for pharmacogenetic studies [4].

A number of SNP recognition techniques have been discussed in a number of reviews that each of them offers several advantages and also suffers from some disadvantages. However, for a large-scale SNP genotype, there is still a need for a precise, cost-effective, and efficient method [5]. The methods for SNP genotyping can be broadly classified into two categories: enzyme-based methods including, oligonucleotide ligation [6], primer extension [7], cleavage [8], endonuclease digestion [9] and hybridization-based methods, such as TaqMan or 5′ nuclease assay, molecular beacon [10], fluorescence resonance energy transfer assay [11,12].

Fluorescence spectroscopy is the particularly interesting method in the study of SNPs, mainly because they offer the advantages of simplicity and sensitivity, without any need for radioactive substances [13]. In many reports, organic fluorescence dyes are used as fluorescence probes for the determination of nucleic acid [14,15]. In comparison with the organic dyes, quantum dots (the semiconductor nanocrystals) offer better spectral properties, such as a broad excitation spectrum, negligible photobleaching, and a tunable, symmetric and narrow emission spectrum. Because of their prominent spectral properties and photochemical stabilities, along with their high quantum yields in the aqueous suspensions, the application of the quantum dots in the determination of biomacromolecules will be a revolutionary development in bioanalysis [16].

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles or carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of nanomaterials that have attracted much attention over the past decade [17,18]. In addition to the above mentioned optical properties, CDs have the desired advantages of low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and low cost. In addition, these nanoparticles are easily synthesized and passive and functionalized surface of CDs allow for the control of their physicochemical properties. CDs have found many applications in the field of chemical sensing [19,20], biosensing and bioimaging [21], DNA sensing [22–24], nanomedicine [25], photocatalysis [26], and electrocatalysis [27].

In the present manuscript, we present a simple, effective, and inexpensive fluorescence assay for genotyping of different SNPs by employing the CDs that have been linked to adenosine, cytidine, guanosine, and thymidine mononucleotides probe using phosphoramidite chemistry through CDs surface amine groups. In the present method, the DNA probe was immobilized on the surface of the gold compact recordable disk. The monobase functionalized carbon dots (MB-CDs)were accumulated on the disk surface via hybridization of monobases with mismatch sites. After binding of MB-CDs to target DNA, the decreases in the fluorescence intensities of residual CDs were followed.

Tris(2‑carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP), Tris–HCl, sodium hydroxide, potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), sodium chloride, 6‑mercaptohexanol (MCH), potassium chloride, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Merck (Germany). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received, without any further purification.

The synthetic oligonucleotides were purchased from Eurofins MWG/Operon Co. with the following sequences (5′ to 3′) and received as the lyophilized powder:
Probe: SH-(CH2) 6-CTG CGT TTT
Com: TGC CGA AAA AAA ACG CAG
A-C mismatch (H1): TAC CGA AAA AAA ACG CAG
G-T Mismatch (H2): TGC TGA AAA AAA ACG CAG
Capture: TTT TCG GCA

The stock solutions of the oligonucleotides were prepared using 1× phosphate buffer solution (containing, 0.01 M Na2HPO4, 2 mM KH2PO4, 0.15 M NaCl and 0.15 M KCl, pH 7.4). Adenosine 5′‑triphosphate (ATP), cytidine 5′‑triphosphate (CTP), guanosine 5′‑triphosphate (GTP), thymidine 5′‑triphosphate (TTP) and DNA polymerase I (Klenow fragment), were purchased Vivantis Co. (Malaysia). The stock solutions of various monobases were diluted to desired concentrations using 20 mM Tris–HCl buffer solution containing 20 mM NaCl (pH = 7).

Optical absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-160). The fluorescence intensities of the solutions were followed by Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrofluorometer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed by ZAHNER ZENNIUM pro potentiostat system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing and Fabrication of the Assay on the Gold Platform
ce:section.ce:para: The gold support was prepared from small pieces of a recordable compact disk with a gold reflective layer (CDtrode), which was secured to the bottom of a Teflon cell with an O-ring [28]. A piece of CD was cut, the protective layer was removed, by putting it in concentrated nitric acid, and it washed with thorough water. Subsequently, the CDtrode was electrochemically cleaned by potential cycling in NaOH (0.01 M) over the range of 0 to −1.5 V and H2SO4 (0.05 M) over the range of 0–+1.5 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in a three-electrode system containing a CDtrode as a working electrode, an Ag/AgCl/3M KCl as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode in a solution containing K3[Fe(CN)6]–K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1, 0.5 mM) and KCl (0.1 M) by applying an AC potential, with signal amplitude of 5 mV and frequency range over 10 kHz to 0.1 Hz, at the open circuit potential.

The thiolated probe was freshly prepared and disulfide bonds were reduced using TCEP solution [29]. In a typical procedure, first, a 20 μL aliquot of 6 μM probe with 5 μL TCEP (0.5 mM) was incubated in a dark place for 1 h and was subsequently dropped on the gold surface and wait for 12 h at room temperature to form a self-assembled monolayer of the DNA probe on the gold surface through Au–S chemistry. The probe-modified surface was then washed using 10 mM PBS (pH 7.40) to remove non-bonded probes on the surface. Afterward, the pinholes and non-covered parts were blocked using 10 μL MCH solution (5 μM) to eliminate non-specific adsorbed DNA molecules. Subsequently, 20 μL of the different concentrations of target oligonucleotides [H1 (A–C) and/or H2 (G–T)] along with 4 μL of 2 M magnesium chloride was dropped on the MCH/probe-modified electrode for 2 h. A control experiment was carried out with a complementary oligonucleotide instead of mismatch targets. After washing this MCH/probe/target-Au surface, an aliquot of 20 μL of the complementary target (12 μM, two-times more concentrated than the probe; 2× Com) oligonucleotide was added to the electrodes and allowed to hybridize with non-hybridized probe molecules for 2 h. In the next step, 20 μL of 12 μM capture (again two-times more concentrated than the probe; 2× capture) oligonucleotide, and 2 mL of 2 M magnesium chloride were placed on Au surfaces for 2 h. The capture is complementary to the nonhybridized section of the target and could be hybridized with this part. To achieve the optimum hybridization times of each step, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Monobase Functionalized Carbon Dots (MB-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized and characterized using a hydrothermal method exactly as same as described in our previous manuscript [30]. The CDs were functionalized by the monobases, adenosine 5′‑triphosphate (A), cytidine 5′‑triphosphate (C), guanosine 5′‑triphosphate (D) and thymidine 5′‑triphosphate (T) solution by the addition of 500 μL CDs suspensions to, 500 μL monobase solutions (5 mM) and shaking for 30 to 270 min [31]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of the CD-MB were recorded using FTIR spectrophotometer model JASCO (Japan). The pH and time for functionalization of CDs with MBs were optimized by following the changes in the fluorescence intensities of the CDs suspensions after incubation with the modified gold surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybridization Between Mismatched Sites of Duplex DNA and MB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Monobase functionalized carbon dots (50 μL), TBS (pH 7.4, 20 mM), and MgCl2 (10 mM), was added to the modified gold surface for 1 h. After 1 h hybridization, the residual suspensions were transferred to quartz cuvette for fluorescence measurement. The fluorescence intensities of MB-CDs residual suspensions were measured before and after hybridization with the modified gold surface.

The capability of hybridization of the nucleobases in mismatch sites with their complementary free monobases can be used for the genotyping of different mismatches. Different monobases are covalently bound to carbon dots as fluorophores and the fluorescence intensities are followed. The sensing strategy of the SNP detection has been illustrated in Scheme 1 . The assay was designed and fabricated as was mentioned in the Experimental section. In the case of presence of mismatch sites in the double-helix structure on the surface, by the addition of monobase functionalized carbon dots, MB-CDs, they are bound to the mismatch sites and the fluorescence intensities of suspensions are decreased. Whereas in the presence complementary target, there are no mismatch sites and so no interaction between MB-CDs and the strands on the surface. Therefore, the fluorescence intensities of the suspensions would not change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing and Fabrication of the Assay
ce:section.ce:para: The thiolated probe is assembled on a gold recordable compact-disk surface and subsequently, hybridizes with the mismatch or complementary target. Finally, the nonhybridized section of the target is hybridized with the capture strand. Each step of the modification can be monitored by using EIS. The immobilization of DNA on the gold surface alters the capacitance and the electron-transfer resistance of the solid support–electrolyte interface. Fig. 1 shows the EIS spectra, using Fe(CN)6 3−/4 − as the redox probe, of the bare gold surface and after each step of the modification. The charge-transfer resistance (Rct) increases upon immobilization and hybridization of each strand on the surface. This is consistent with the electrostatic repulsion between the redox probe and negative charges and spatial restrictions of the DNA on the gold surface. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results show that charge transfer resistances are increased by increasing the probe immobilization time up to 12 h and then it remains roughly constant. Also, the hybridization times for hybridization of the probe strand with target and capture strands were optimized. The impedance spectra show that 2 h are enough as the hybridization time (Fig. 1S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Monobase Functionalized Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal method as previously described by Zhu et al. [32] and characterized as described in our previous study [30]. Briefly, 40 mL solution including 4 g citric acid and 1 mL ethylene diamine was heated to 250 °C in water in a Teflon-lined autoclave for 5 h. Subsequently, the solution was cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min to remove aggregated nanodots. The supernatant suspension was characterized using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. FTIR spectrum confirms the presence of amine function groups on the surface of carbon dots. These functional groups impart excellent dispersibility of carbon dots in water and provide appropriate conditions for the conjugation of CDs with monobases. According to TEM images and DLS results, synthesized CDs have diameters of ~2.5–10 nm. The obtained quantum yield for synthesized CDs was 0.54. In this study, the excitation wavelength of 400 nm was applied and a fluorescence spectrum with maximum intensity at 470 nm was recorded.

Monobase functionalized carbon dots (MB-CDs), such as C-CD, G-CD, T-CD, and A-CD, were obtained by covalent binding of the corresponding single nucleotide to the surface of CDs through phosphoramidite bond formed by the 5′ phosphate group of the single nucleotide with the free amino groups on the carbon dot surfaces [31,33,34]. MB-CDs were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy and the presence of P-O and P-N groups was confirmed by the observed peaks at 500 to 1000 cm−1 peaks (Fig. 2 ) [35]. The experimental conditions including, pH and binding time for the covalent binding between GTP and CDs were optimized by following the differences between fluorescence intensities before (F0) and after (F) incubation of G-CDs suspensions with A-C mismatch site (H1). According to Fig. 2S, the optimum pH of 6.5 and the optimum binding time of 2 h were obtained. A similar optimization procedure for the other nucleotides was performed and similar optimum conditions were obtained.

In summary, a sensitive spectrofluorimetric assay for the detection and genotyping of single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) using carbon dots as fluorescent probe has been described. The presented assay, compared with previously reported fluorescence [14,37–40], is easy to use, affordable, sensitive and selective, with the ability for the detection and genotyping of various SNPs. The synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanodots is simple, easily and environmental friendly and they offer very good quantum efficiency making them excellent alternatives for organic dyes and inorganic quantum dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this study by the Research Council of the University of Isfahan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.08.001.
